title: Equivariant Floer Theory for Symplectic C*-manifolds
speaker: Alexander Ritter
abstract: The talk will be on recent progress in a series of joint papers with Filip Živanović, about a large class of non-compact symplectic manifolds, which includes semiprojective toric manifolds, quiver varieties, and conical symplectic resolutions of singularities. These manifolds admit a Hamiltonian circle action which is part of a pseudo-holomorphic action of a complex torus. The symplectic form on these spaces is highly non-exact, yet we can make sense of Hamiltonian Floer cohomology for functions of the moment map of the circle action. We showed that Floer theory induces a filtration by ideals on quantum cohomology. I will explain recent progress on equivariant Floer cohomology for these spaces, in which case we obtain a filtration on equivariant quantum cohomology. If time permits, I will also mention a presentation of symplectic cohomology and quantum cohomology for semiprojective toric manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Continuation kernels in Hamiltonian Floer theory
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Hamiltonian Floer cohomology on noncompact symplectic manifolds, the continuation map from ordinary cohomology, its kernel, the positive quotient complex, and the direct-limit symplectic cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Slope filtrations by quantum ideals
proof_stages.summary: For a compatible family of Hamiltonians with increasing slope, the kernels of continuation maps form an increasing filtration of ordinary/quantum cohomology, and a pair-of-pants compatibility argument shows these subspaces are quantum ideals.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic C^*-manifold control at infinity
proof_stages.summary: The speaker replaces the usual convex exact-at-infinity setup by symplectic C^*-manifolds, where the S^1 moment map and a holomorphic map to a positive symplectization control Hamiltonian orbits and Floer cylinders at infinity.

proof_stages.label: Morse-Bott spectral sequence and rotation-class localization
proof_stages.summary: A Morse-Bott filtration of the Floer complex computes Floer and symplectic cohomology from families of one-periodic orbits, interprets the kernel filtration as the stage at which ordinary classes are killed, and identifies symplectic cohomology as a quotient/localization of quantum cohomology by the rotation class.

proof_stages.label: Equivariant theories, mixed actions, and Floer localization
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing the three S^1-equivariant theories and classical formality/localization, the talk introduces mixed actions combining the manifold S^1-action and loop reparametrization and proves that for free weights the localized equivariant Floer theory sees only constant orbits, hence equals localized equivariant quantum cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Equivariant continuation filtrations
proof_stages.summary: In the E^+ theory kernel filtrations behave like the non-equivariant ones, while in the Borel E^- theory localization and formality imply injectivity of all continuation maps, forcing a new filtration defined by u-divisibility after continuation.

proof_stages.label: Adjugate embeddings and filtration invariants
proof_stages.summary: Using Seidel-type identifications, adjugate maps over K[[u]], and the resulting negative powers of u, the speaker constructs Young-diagram invariants, filtration polynomials, and equivalent Hilbert-Poincare series measuring the equivariant continuation maps.

proof_stages.label: Computed filtration polynomials and toric directions
proof_stages.summary: The talk reports explicit or partial filtration-polynomial computations for C, O(-1), and T^*CP^1, and ends by pointing to ongoing work on semiprojective toric examples.

examples.name: McKay correspondence for resolutions of C^n/G
examples.note: The earlier Floer-theoretic approach to the cohomological McKay correspondence motivates studying kernels of continuation maps and C^*-actions on noncompact resolutions.
examples.engagement_reason: The McKay correspondence for resolutions of C^n/G is used as motivation and its known/coarse Floer-theoretic features are stated, but no concrete resolution or group case is computed.

examples.note: C^n illustrates the familiar convex/contact-boundary model, while C later gives the simplest filtration polynomial with one invariant factor u^k.
examples.engagement_reason: C^n is described as the convex model with sphere contact hypersurface and r=|z|^2, and later the case C is explicitly assigned filtration polynomials, k new classes, and continuation map invariant factor u^k.

examples.name: A_2 resolution C^2/(Z/3)
examples.note: The quotient resolution with two intersecting CP^1s makes the C^*-equivariant map psi concrete and later visualizes the Morse-Bott spectral sequence and deaths of ordinary cohomology classes.
examples.engagement_reason: For the A_2 resolution the speaker traces the quotient action, Veronese embedding/blow-up, two CP^1 exceptional curves, constructs the equivariant map using x^6, y^6, x^3y^3, and analyzes its Morse–Bott spectral-sequence columns and cancellations.

examples.engagement_reason: For O(-1) over CP^n/blow-up of C^2 the speaker computes dimensions/drop of symplectic cohomology and later gives the equivariant decomposition, matrix diag(1,u^k), and corresponding filtration data.

examples.name: Circle rotating on itself
examples.note: The S^1 action on S^1 is used to display the three classical equivariant theories E^-, E^infty, and E^+ and to check localization when the fixed locus is empty.
examples.engagement_reason: The circle rotating on itself is worked through via a height-function Morse/Borel model, with min/max generators, u-powers, differentials, resulting cohomologies, and localization interpretation.

examples.name: Cotangent bundle T^*CP^1
examples.note: This first nontrivial computation shows that the equivariant Floer module need not be the naive grading shift, with one class becoming unexpectedly u^2-divisible.
examples.engagement_reason: T^*CP^1 is analyzed as a nontrivial example: the speaker discusses slope one, index shift, actual versus naive module structure, u-divisibility, Laurent-series symplectic cohomology, and possible filtration polynomials/continuation matrices.
